coredata.dc:title: Metal ion (Fe3+ and Cu2+) catalyzed synthesis of high quantum yield carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Jiao, Ting
Wen, Haoxiang
Dong, Wenxia
Wen, Wen
Li, Zhongping
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a high quantum yield CDs has been developed by polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) and ascorbic acid (AA) as green facile carbon source in presence of metal ions (Fe3+ or Cu2+). But in the characterization TEM, FTIR, XRD and XPS, the existence of metal was not found in the CDs structure. Therefore, we believe that metal ions play a role of catalyst in the reaction process. The as-prepared CDs had quite high quantum yield (QY) of 39.74% (Fe-CDs) and 44.42% (Cu-CDs), respectively. And the Cu-CDs showed excellent optical stability and salt tolerance with excitation and emission wavelength at 350 nm and 440 nm. The Cu-CDs were used for highly sensitive detection tetracycline (TC), with a good linear relationship in the range of 0.1–50 μmol/L and the detection limit was 26 nmol/L. It also showed potential application value in the field of solid-state lighting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Metal ion catalysis
Carbon dots
Sensors
Tetracycline
Solid-state lighting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of fabrication and application of CDs doped Fe3+ and Cu2+.
(a) The fluorescence intensity of CDs, Fe-CDs and Cu-CDs varies with time. (b) Excitation and emission spectra of CDs, Fe-CDs and Cu-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, Fe-CDs and Cu-CDs before and after adding TC (0.01 mol/L). (b) Fluorescence spectra of Cu-CDs varies with pH (c) The effect of different concentrations of NaCl on Cu-CDs fluorescence intensity (D) The storage stability of Cu-CDs.

head.ce:title: Metal ion (Fe3+ and Cu2+) catalyzed synthesis of high quantum yield carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a high quantum yield CDs has been developed by polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) and ascorbic acid (AA) as green facile carbon source in presence of metal ions (Fe3+ or Cu2+). But in the characterization TEM, FTIR, XRD and XPS, the existence of metal was not found in the CDs structure. Therefore, we believe that metal ions play a role of catalyst in the reaction process. The as-prepared CDs had quite high quantum yield (QY) of 39.74% (Fe-CDs) and 44.42% (Cu-CDs), respectively. And the Cu-CDs showed excellent optical stability and salt tolerance with excitation and emission wavelength at 350 nm and 440 nm. The Cu-CDs were used for highly sensitive detection tetracycline (TC), with a good linear relationship in the range of 0.1–50 μmol/L and the detection limit was 26 nmol/L. It also showed potential application value in the field of solid-state lighting.

ce:sections.ce:para: A series of sensors based on carbon dots (CDs) has witnessed tremendous growth in multiple applications such as environment monitoring, electrocatalysis, gene delivery, drug, anti-counterfeiting, bioimaging and environmental fields [1–3].

However, starting from the fundamental factors of application, researchers have been pursuing carbon quantum dots with high fluorescence quantum yield, and hope to develop more green and convenient methods [4–6]. Usually, with N, O, S, and P doped in CDs can introduce holes or electron, which distinctly enhance the fluorescence yield, improve the electronic structure and chemical activity dots [7]. However, compared to doping with nonmetals, metal ions endow more fragile electrons and unoccupied orbitals (especially for transition metal ions), their atomic radius also large than nonmetallic heteroatoms. The doping of metal ions facilitated the radiation recombination of electrons on the CDs surface, thereby increasing the quantum yield [8]. Since the first composites of heteroatom-doped CDs using Zn2+ ions (ZnO and ZnS) as precursors was successfully synthesized by Sun and his coworkers [9], several novel metal doped materials, such as Cu-CDs [10], Mn-CDs [11], Co/C-dots [12], Gd-CQDs [13], Fe-CDs were continuously reported. However, the latest research has found that during the synthesis of carbon quantum dots, the presence of metal ions was not necessarily doped into the structure of carbon quantum dots, metal ions may also act as a catalyst to promote the formation of carbon quantum dots with less time [14].

Herein, a cyan fluorescent CDs with high quantum yield properties was synthesized in one step reaction, used polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) and ascorbic acid (AA) as green reagents and metal ion (Fe3+ or Cu2+) as catalysts. The prepared carbon dots were used as a simple, selective fluorescence sensor for TC detection and with good light-emitting behavior in solid state.

Basically, cyan fluorescent CDs were synthesized in a facile, mild and one step route. Highly fluorescent CDs were obtained by reaction at 80 °C for 12 h using AA and PVP as raw source (Fig. 1 ). The color of the solution gradually changed from colorless to yellow under daylight. The as-prepared CDs emitted bright cyan fluorescence under the ultraviolet light. When metal ion Fe3+ and Cu2+ doped into CDs, the luminescence properties were effectively enhanced.

As a mild reducing agent, AA is easy to react with metal ions, especially in the presence of metal Fe3+ and Cu2+ that accelerate the oxidation of AA [15]. It can be seen that Cu2+ displayed higher catalytic efficiency than Fe3+ (Fig. 2 a). As the reaction time reached to 8 h, the QY of Fe-CDs improved from 9.83 % to 39.74 % (Table S 1). The Cu-CDs obtained the highest fluorescence intensity in only 4 h, the QY also increased to 44.42 %, which was far better than most water-soluble CDs. The addition of Fe3+ and Cu2+ not only enhanced the fluorescence intensity, but also extended the fluorescence lifetime (Fig. S 1). Fe-CDs and Cu-CDs extended the lifetime of CDs from 2.71 ns to 2.86 ns and 3.04 ns, respectively. Nevertheless, the CDs, Fe-CDs and Cu-CDs obtained the same fluorescent excitation and the emission state, almost of which located in 350 nm of excitation and 440 nm of emission (Fig. 2 b). In the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S 2), there was no localized surface plasmon resonance peak of bulk particles appeared in long wavelengths, indicating a comparatively uniform surface state of CDs and after mixed metal ion. In conclusion, we develop a novel strategy based the interaction of Fe3+ and Cu2+ with AA, the fluorescence quantum yield can be ingenious increased. It also strongly indicated that the superiority of metal as catalysts for moderate fluorescence properties.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) characterized the morphology and structure of CDs, Fe-CDs and Cu-CDs (Fig. S 3 a-c). CDs were spherical particles with a particle size less than 10 nm. The CDs obtained were spherical particles with an average diameter about 5.5 nm. In the TEM image, obvious crystal lattice about 0.21 nm can be seen, belonged to the C (100) crystal plane [3,16]. Fe-CDs and Cu-CDs, both are close in size to CDs, and they also have similar lattice. Although metal was added during the preparation of CDs, no trace of metal element was found in the characterization results, which could be attributed to its tiny content and the reduction during the dialysis. As shown in XPS pattern (Fig. S 3 d), the three curves of CDs, Fe-CDs and Cu-CDs exhibited almost the same distribution patterns of C, N and O element content. Consistent with the result of XPS, FTIR and XRD pattern also showed the similar phenomenon between the CDs, Fe-CDs and Cu-CDs (Fig. S 4 and Fig. S 5).

The CDs-based sensor for detecting TC was established based on the quenching effect. When 10 μL TC was added, the fluorescence of CDs, Fe-CDs and Cu-CDs decreased differently (Fig. 3 a). Compared with the CDs and Fe-CDs, the quench of Cu-CDs had the most obvious effect, therefore, the Cu-CD was chosen as a sensor for highly sensitive detection of TC. The fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs in the buffer solution at pH 2–12 was remarkably different (Fig. 3 b). Obviously, under acidic condition especially the pH is 5, it was more conducive to strong fluorescence, the result consistent with the inset under 365 nm UV light. In addition, the prepared Cu-CDs have good salt tolerance (Fig. 3 c), and the quenching of the fluorescence intensity changed weakly after several months of storage (Fig. 3 d), indicating Cu-CDs showed unique photobleaching resistance and optical stability.

Here, the Cu-CDs exhibited a great selective fluorescence quenching response towards TC. When the concentrations of TC increased in the range of 0.1–50 μmol/L, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually, a good linear relationship between F0/F and the concentrations of TC were obtained in a narrow range. The linear equation can be expressed as y = 0.9724 + 0.01608x, the correlation coefficient was calculated to be 0.9945 and the detection limit was 26 nmol/L (S/N = 3) (Fig. S 6 a). The reported fluorescence quenching mechanisms mainly included photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE). In the UV pattern of TC, however, has clear overlap with the excitation spectrum of CDs, and the fluorescence lifetimes before and after adding of TC changed from 2.77 ns to 2.83 ns, basically there was not much change (Fig. S 6b, c). Therefore, the origin of the Cu-CDs to detect TC based the inner filter effect (IFE) [17]. There was no significant fluorescence change in the presence of other antibiotics (Fig. S 6 d). These results indicated the Cu-CDs have high selectivity towards TC. The recovery rate ranged from 94% to 107.8%, when different concentrations of TC standard samples were added to the actual water (Table S 2) with RSD 4.19%-8.57%. In addition, we compared with previous reports about heteroatom doped CDs related to quantum yield and applications (Table S 3).

The luminescence properties of carbon quantum dots in solid media have always been a research hotspot in the field of optical materials. Cu-CDs doped fluorescent agarose hydrogels, inks and powder (Fig. S 7) exhibited strong cyan fluorescence and good application in solid-state lighting fields with a bright future.

In summary, a novel CDs platform was established based on the catalytic effect of metal ions. Furthermore, the Cu-CDs exhibited a great potential in the field of antibiotic detection and solid-state lighting fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Ting Jiao: Writing – original draft. Haoxiang Wen: Validation. Wenxia Dong: Writing-review. Wen Wen: Writing-review. Zhongping Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575083), Jinzhong College “1331 Project” Innovation Team (jzxycxtd2019007), the 12th“Hundred Talents Plan” in Shanxi Province (128,129) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi province (20210302123494).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2022.132575.
